_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I see the flow:

1. When entering ProxyActivity:
   - main() runs: withClash { queryOverride }, withClash { queryProxyGroupNames } - two Binder calls
   - ProxyDesign(...) is created - in its init block it inflates layout, sets up ViewPager2 with ProxyPageAdapter, creates List(groupNames.size) { ProxyAdapter(...) } - so N adapters are created
   - setContentDesign(design) - sets the view
   - design.requests.send(ProxyDesign.Request.ReloadAll) - immediately sends ReloadAll
   - The select loop receives ReloadAll and then trySends Reload(0), Reload(1), ... Reload(N-1) for every group
   - Each Reload is handled by launch { withClash { queryProxyGroup(...) }; design.updateGroup(...) }
   - So we launch N coroutines almost at once, each doing a Binder call (queryProxyGroup) and then updateGroup which updates the adapter on Main

The stutter could be caused by:
1. **ReloadAll right after setContentDesign**: We're sending ReloadAll immediately, so all N groups start loading at once. Each updateGroup does adapter.updateAdapter which likely does notifyDataSetChanged or similar on the RecyclerView inside the ViewPager page. So we get N updates in quick succession, all on Main thread - that could cause jank.

2. **Too many concurrent reloads**: names.indices.forEach { design.requests.trySend(ProxyDesign.Request.Reload(it)) } sends N reload requests. They're processed in the select loop - each one launches a new coroutine. So we have N coroutines running. The Semaphore(10) limits to 10 concurrent withClash { queryProxyGroup } calls. So up to 10 groups load in parallel, each then calling design.updateGroup. So we get up to 10 UI updates (updateGroup -> updateAdapter -> notify) in quick succession. That could cause frame drops.

3. **ProxyDesign init**: Creating ProxyPageAdapter with N ProxyAdapters, TabLayoutMediator.attach(), etc. might do some work on the main thread. But the main culprit is likely the burst of Reload requests.

Solutions:
1. **Defer ReloadAll**: Don't send ReloadAll immediately. Post it to the next frame or after a short delay so the activity has time to draw the initial layout. Then the user sees the screen first, then data fills in.

2. **Stagger the reloads**: Instead of sending all Reload(0), Reload(1), ... at once, send them with a small delay (e.g. 50ms between each) or only load the first visible page initially and load others when they're about to be visible (lazy load). That would reduce the burst of Main thread work.

3. **Load visible tab first**: When we send ReloadAll, we could first only send Reload(initialPosition) so the visible tab loads first, then send the rest with a delay. So the user sees the first screen populate quickly, then other tabs load in the background.

I will implement: defer ReloadAll by one frame (post to handler) so the layout is drawn first, and optionally stagger the reload requests so we don't do 10+ updateGroup calls in the same frame. Actually the simplest fix is to post ReloadAll so we don't block the first frame. And we could stagger: when handling ReloadAll, instead of forEach { trySend(Reload(it)) }, we could launch a coroutine that sends Reload(0), delay, Reload(1), delay, ... so the UI updates are spread out. But that might make the initial load feel slower (user sees empty then one by one fills). A better approach: load the current page first (Reload(initialPosition)), then load the rest with small delays. So we need to know initialPosition when handling ReloadAll - we don't have it in the activity. We have binding.pagesView.currentItem in the design. So in the activity we could send ReloadAll and in the design/activity when handling ReloadAll we first send Reload(currentItem) then the rest. Actually the simplest is to just defer ReloadAll by one frame: design.requests.send(ReloadAll) is called right after setContentDesign. So the view is set, then we enter the select loop, then we receive ReloadAll. So the first frame might already be drawing. The issue is that we then immediately trySend N Reload requests, and they all get processed - each launches a coroutine. So in the same "tick" we launch N coroutines. They compete for the Semaphore(10), so 10 start, and as each finishes it calls design.updateGroup which runs on Main (updateGroup is a suspend function that calls adapter.updateAdapter - need to check if that switches to Main). I will check updateGroup - it doesn't explicitly use withContext(Main), so it runs in the calling coroutine's context. The launch { } in the activity uses the activity's scope which might be Main. So when we do launch { withClash { queryProxyGroup }; design.updateGroup() }, the withClash runs on Dispatchers.IO (from withClash default), and updateGroup runs after that - in which context? The launch doesn't specify a dispatcher, so it uses the scope's dispatcher. BaseActivity is MainScope(), so launch uses Dispatchers.Main. So the flow is: Main thread runs launch block, withClash switches to IO for the block, so queryProxyGroup runs on IO. Then we're back on Main for design.updateGroup(). So all the updateGroup calls happen on Main, and they can pile up if 10 coroutines finish around the same time. So we get 10 adapter updates in quick succession on Main - that's the jank.

So the fix: stagger the Reload requests so we don't have 10 updateGroup calls in the same frame. We could use a coroutine that sends Reload(0), delay(16), Reload(1), delay(16), ... so we spread the load over time. Or we could limit concurrency of the updateGroup part - but the real issue is the burst of Binder calls and then the burst of UI updates. I will try: 1) Defer ReloadAll by one frame using Handler.post or view.post, so the initial layout is drawn. 2) When processing ReloadAll, stagger the Reload requests - e.g. launch a coroutine that for (i in names.indices) { design.requests.trySend(Reload(i)); delay(32) } so we send one reload every 32ms. That way the first group loads and updates, then 32ms later the second, etc. The user would see the first tab populate quickly (if it's the current one), then others fill in. Actually if we have 5 groups and we send Reload(0) first, then delay 32, then Reload(1), etc. - the visible tab might be 0, so we'd see group 0 load first. Good. But 5 * 32 = 160ms total before all are sent - and the actual loading is async so we'd have loading happening over 160ms + load time. That might feel smoother.

I will implement staggered reload: when we receive ReloadAll, we launch a coroutine that sends Reload requests with a small delay between each. So we need to use launch { for (i in names.indices) { design.requests.trySend(ProxyDesign.Request.Reload(i)); kotlinx.coroutines.delay(40) } }. That way we don't flood the channel with all at once. But the receiver still processes them one by one - so we'd get Reload(0), process it (launch coroutine A), Reload(1), process it (launch coroutine B), ... So the reloads are already staggered in terms of when they're sent. The issue is that coroutines A, B, C, ... might complete around the same time if the Binder calls take similar time. So we'd still get a burst of updateGroup. To really stagger the UI updates we could add a delay inside the Reload handler before calling updateGroup - but that would delay the display. Alternatively we could make the Reload handler delay between processing each one - but that would require serializing the Reload processing. Actually the simplest fix is to just defer the initial ReloadAll so the first frame draws: use binding.root.post { design.requests.trySend(ProxyDesign.Request.ReloadAll) } or in the activity, after setContentDesign(design), do design.root.post { design.requests.trySend(ProxyDesign.Request.ReloadAll) }. So we don't send ReloadAll synchronously - we post it. That way the view is attached and the first frame can draw (empty state), then ReloadAll is processed. So we avoid doing the burst of work in the same frame as the initial layout. I will try that first.

And we could also stagger the Reload sends: in the ReloadAll handler, instead of names.indices.forEach { trySend(Reload(it)) }, we could use launch { for (i in names.indices) { trySend(Reload(i)); delay(50) } }. So we spread sending over 50ms * N. That way the reload coroutines don't all start at once, so they're less likely to all complete at once. So we get smoother UI updates.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I understand the flow:

1. In `ProxyActivity.main()`:
   - It fetches `mode` and `names` (proxy group names) via `withClash`
   - Creates `ProxyDesign` and calls `setContentDesign(design)`
   - Immediately sends `design.requests.send(ProxyDesign.Request.ReloadAll)`

2. When `ReloadAll` is received:
   - It does `names.indices.forEach { idx -> design.requests.trySend(ProxyDesign.Request.Reload(idx)) }`
   - So it sends **all** Reload requests at once (one per proxy group)

3. Each `Reload` is handled by launching a coroutine that:
   - Acquires `reloadLock.withPermit` (semaphore 10, so up to 10 concurrent)
   - Calls `withClash { queryProxyGroup(...) }` - this is a blocking/suspend call to the service
   - Then calls `design.updateGroup(...)` which calls `adapter.updateAdapter(...)` on the main thread

So the problem is:
- **Many groups** → many Reload requests sent at once
- Up to 10 concurrent coroutines each doing `queryProxyGroup` and then `updateGroup`
- Each `updateGroup` triggers `adapter.updateAdapter(position, ...)` which likely updates the ViewPager's page adapters and their RecyclerViews
- All these updates can flood the main thread and cause jank

Solutions:
1. **Stagger the Reload requests**: Instead of sending all `Reload(idx)` at once, send them with a small delay between each (e.g. 20–50 ms), so UI updates are spread over time.
2. **Limit concurrency**: Reduce the semaphore from 10 to 2 or 3, so fewer groups load at once.
3. **Defer ReloadAll**: Don't send ReloadAll immediately. Post it to the next frame or after a short delay so the initial layout can complete first.
4. **Load visible first**: Load the current tab's group first, then load others with delay.

I will look at `updateGroup` / `updateAdapter` to see how heavy the UI update is.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I have a clear picture:

1. **ProxyActivity.main()**:
   - Sends `ReloadAll` immediately after `setContentDesign(design)`.
   - On `ReloadAll`, it does `names.indices.forEach { design.requests.trySend(ProxyDesign.Request.Reload(idx)) }` — **all Reload requests at once**.
   - There's a semaphore of 10, so up to 10 coroutines run in parallel.

2. Each **Reload** coroutine:
   - Calls `withClash { queryProxyGroup(...) }` (IPC + core work).
   - Calls `design.updateGroup(...)` which:
     - In `ProxyPageAdapter.updateAdapter`: does `proxies.map { ProxyViewState(...) }` on Default dispatcher (good).
     - Then on Main: `adapters[position].swapDataSet(...)` which does `notifyItemRangeChanged` or remove+insert.
     - Then `requestRedrawVisible()` which does `recyclerView.invalidateChildren()`.

So when there are e.g. 8 proxy groups:
- 8 Reload messages are sent at once.
- Up to 10 (actually 8) coroutines start, each does queryProxyGroup then updateGroup.
- They all hit the Main dispatcher around the same time: each does swapDataSet (notifyItemRangeChanged) + invalidateChildren for one page.
- The ViewPager2 has multiple pages; each page has a RecyclerView. So we get many Main-thread updates in quick succession → jank.

**Fix strategy:**
1. **Stagger Reload requests**: When handling `ReloadAll`, don't send all `Reload(idx)` at once. Send them with a small delay (e.g. 50ms) between each, so UI updates are spread. We need to do this in ProxyActivity.
2. **Optional: Load visible tab first**: Send Reload(currentPage) first, then others with delay. That way the user sees the first screen populated quickly.

I will implement staggering in ProxyActivity:
- When we receive ReloadAll, we could use `launch` and inside it loop with `delay(50); design.requests.send(Reload(idx))` for each index.
- Or we could send Reload(0), Reload(1), ... with a delay between each. The key is to not flood the channel.

Actually re-reading the code: the semaphore already limits to 10 concurrent. The issue is that all 10 (or N) start almost at once, so they all finish around the same time and all call updateGroup → Main thread gets many swapDataSet + requestRedrawVisible in a burst.

So staggering the **sending** of Reload requests will naturally stagger when each coroutine starts, and thus when each updateGroup hits Main. So we do:

```kotlin
ProxyDesign.Request.ReloadAll -> {
    launch {
        names.indices.forEach { idx ->
            design.requests.send(ProxyDesign.Request.Reload(idx))
            if (idx < names.size - 1) delay(50) // or 30ms
        }
    }
}
```

We need to add `delay` and `launch` - delay is from kotlinx.coroutines. I will check the imports in ProxyActivity.

Actually we already have `launch` in the when branch for `Reload`. So we need to add a delay between sending Reload requests. I'll use 40ms or 50ms so that e.g. 10 groups = 450ms total to send, and they'll be spread out.

Another option: reduce semaphore from 10 to 2. Then only 2 groups load at a time. But that might make the "full load" slower. Staggering is better UX: first group appears quickly, then others fill in.

I will also consider: load the **current** tab first. The ViewPager's current item might be 0 initially, or from uiStore.proxyLastGroup. So we could send Reload(initialPosition) first, then Reload(0), Reload(1), ... with delay, skipping initialPosition. That way the visible tab loads first.

I'll implement:
1. Stagger: when ReloadAll, launch a coroutine that sends Reload(0), delay(40), Reload(1), delay(40), ...
2. Optionally prioritize the "last used" group: get initialPosition = names.indexOf(uiStore.proxyLastGroup).coerceAtLeast(0), send Reload(initialPosition) first (no delay), then for other indices in order with delay. This way the tab user usually sees loads first.

I will check - in ProxyActivity we have access to uiStore and names. We have names.indexOf(uiStore.proxyLastGroup) - but that might be -1. So initialPosition = names.indexOf(uiStore.proxyLastGroup).let { if (it >= 0) it else 0 }. So we send Reload(initialPosition) first, then for idx in names.indices where idx != initialPosition we send with delay. So:

```kotlin
ProxyDesign.Request.ReloadAll -> {
    launch {
        val priority = names.indexOf(uiStore.proxyLastGroup).let { if (it >= 0) it else 0 }
        design.requests.send(ProxyDesign.Request.Reload(priority))
        names.indices.forEach { idx ->
            if (idx != priority) {
                delay(45)
                design.requests.send(ProxyDesign.Request.Reload(idx))
            }
        }
    }
}
```

Wait, we're in the main loop, we're not in a coroutine scope that we can launch from... I will look again. We have `launch { ... }` in the `is ProxyDesign.Request.Reload` branch. So we're inside `design.requests.onReceive { ... }` and the block is a suspend lambda? I will check - we have `while (isActive) { select<Unit> { ... } }`. So we're in a coroutine. And in the when (it) we have `launch { ... }` for Reload. So we do have a scope. So for ReloadAll we can do `launch { ... delay() ... }`. We need to add `kotlinx.coroutines.delay` import.

I will implement this.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


Identifying the cause: entering the proxy screen sends `ReloadAll`, which immediately enqueues a `Reload(idx)` for every group. Up to 10 load in parallel (semaphore 10), so many `updateGroup` → `swapDataSet` + `requestRedrawVisible()` run on the main thread at once and cause jank.

Staggering Reload requests and loading the visible (last-used) tab first.

_**Agent (default)**_


针对代理界面进入时的卡顿，做了两处修改：

**原因简述：**  
进入代理界面会立刻发 `ReloadAll`，然后对**所有**代理组一次性发出 `Reload(idx)`。之前用 `Semaphore(10)`，最多 10 个组同时加载；每个加载完都会在主线程做 `updateGroup`（`swapDataSet` + `requestRedrawVisible()`），主线程在短时间内被大量更新刷屏，造成卡顿。

**修改内容：**

1. **错峰发送 Reload，并优先当前可见页**（`ProxyActivity.kt`）  
   - 收到 `ReloadAll` 后，在 `launch` 里按顺序发送 `Reload(idx)`，不再一次性 `trySend` 全部。  
   - 先发当前/上次使用的分组：`priority = names.indexOf(uiStore.proxyLastGroup).let { if (it >= 0) it else 0 }`，先 `send(Reload(priority))`，这样当前看到的 Tab 会先加载完。  
   - 其余分组每隔约 **45ms** 再发下一个 `Reload(idx)`，把主线程上的更新在时间上摊开，减轻卡顿。

2. **降低并发加载数量**  
   - 将 `reloadLock` 从 `Semaphore(10)` 改为 `Semaphore(3)`，同一时刻最多 3 个组在加载，主线程上 `updateGroup` 的并发度降低，减少集中刷新。

效果是：  
- 当前/上次选中的代理组会最先加载完，界面更快可用；  
- 其他组在几十毫秒间隔内陆续加载，主线程压力分散，进入代理界面时的卡顿会明显减轻。

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I understand the flow:

1. **LogcatDesign**:
   - Has `adapter.messages` - the list of LogMessage
   - `patchMessages(messages, removed, appended)` - updates the adapter with new messages. For streaming it uses reverseLayout/stackFromEnd so newest is at bottom.
   - For **local file** (streaming=false): LinearLayoutManager has default reverseLayout=false, so messages are shown in list order (index 0 at top). The messages from LogcatReader.readAll() are probably in time order (oldest first).
   - For **streaming**: reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true so newest at bottom.

2. User wants: "点击之后日志顺序和当前顺序相反（时间顺序）" - when clicked, the log order is reversed (time order). So we need a toggle button that reverses the current display order.

Options:
- **A)** Store a "sort ascending/descending" flag. When toggled, reverse the list and update the adapter (e.g. replace the list with list.reversed(), then notify). Also we may need to flip RecyclerView's reverseLayout so that scroll position and "stick to bottom" behavior make sense.
- **B)** Just reverse the list in the adapter and call notifyDataSetChanged or diff and update. And set reverseLayout to the opposite.

For **streaming mode**: currently reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true → newest at bottom. If we "reverse", we want oldest at bottom → reverseLayout=false, stackFromEnd=false? And reverse the messages list so that we show newest first (index 0 = newest). So we'd set reverseLayout=false, stackFromEnd=false and pass messages.reversed() so adapter shows newest first. When toggled again, we go back to reverseLayout=true and original order.

Actually re-reading the user request: "日志顺序和当前顺序相反" = "the log order is the opposite of the current order". So one click = reverse. So we need:
1. A toggle button (e.g. sort icon) in the toolbar
2. State: isReversed (or timeOrder: NewestFirst / OldestFirst)
3. On click: flip the state, reverse the displayed list, and update RecyclerView layout (reverseLayout) so that the list display matches.

Implementation:
- In LogcatDesign: add `var sortNewestFirst: Boolean` - for streaming we start with newest at bottom (which is like oldest-first in terms of list index 0 = oldest). Actually I will think in terms of "display order":
  - Current streaming: reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true → items are laid out from bottom, so item 0 is at bottom, last item at top. So visually: top = newest, bottom = oldest. So "newest first" (top to bottom).
  - Current local file: reverseLayout=false → item 0 at top. So if messages are oldest-first, we have top=oldest, bottom=newest.

So I will define:
- **Newest first**: newest at top (index 0 = newest)
- **Oldest first**: oldest at top (index 0 = oldest)

When user clicks "reverse order":
- If currently newest first → switch to oldest first (reverse the list, and set reverseLayout false, stackFromEnd false for streaming)
- If currently oldest first → switch to newest first

Actually the simplest is: we don't store "newest first" vs "oldest first". We just store "reversed" (from default). Default for streaming: newest at top (reverseLayout true + stackFromEnd gives that). Default for file: probably oldest at top (index 0 = first in file). So:
- **reversed** = false: show in default order (streaming: newest at top; file: oldest at top)
- **reversed** = true: show in opposite order (streaming: oldest at top; file: newest at top)

So on click we:
1. reversed = !reversed
2. adapter.messages = adapter.messages.reversed()
3. Update LinearLayoutManager: reverseLayout = if (streaming) !reversed else reversed (need to think)
4. notify the adapter - full refresh or reverse list and notifyItemRangeChanged

I will think again for streaming:
- reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true: the RecyclerView lays out items so that the last item in the adapter is at the bottom of the screen. So adapter[0] is at top, adapter[last] at bottom. So we want adapter order = [newest, ..., oldest] to have newest at top. So we already have that if we feed messages with newest first. But wait, in streaming, patchMessages appends to the end (appended). So adapter.messages has oldest first (index 0 = oldest). With reverseLayout=true, the first item (oldest) goes to... I will check. reverseLayout in LinearLayoutManager: when true, items are laid out from end to start. So position 0 is at the bottom. So adapter[0]=oldest at bottom, adapter[n-1]=newest at top. So currently: top=newest, bottom=oldest. So we have "newest first" (newest at top). Good.
- If we reverse: we want oldest at top. So we set reverseLayout=false, stackFromEnd=false, and adapter.messages = messages.reversed() so adapter[0]=newest... no, reversed() would make adapter[0]=newest (was last), adapter[last]=oldest. So with reverseLayout=false, adapter[0] at top = newest. That's still newest at top. To get oldest at top we need adapter[0]=oldest, so we want adapter.messages = messages (no reverse). So "reversed" display = oldest at top means we need reverseLayout=false and adapter order = oldest first (current order). So:
- Default (reversed=false): reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true, messages order = oldest first (as now) → display newest at top.
- Reversed (reversed=true): reverseLayout=false, stackFromEnd=false, messages order = oldest first → display oldest at top.

So toggling "reversed" we only need to change reverseLayout and stackFromEnd, and scroll position. We don't need to reverse the list - we just flip the layout! So for streaming:
- reversed = false: reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true
- reversed = true: reverseLayout=false, stackFromEnd=false

And we need to reverse the list for display when reversed=true... No wait. With reverseLayout=false and same list (oldest first), we get adapter[0]=oldest at top. So we don't reverse the list; we just change reverseLayout and stackFromEnd. So the same adapter.messages, just flip the layout manager. So we only need to set reverseLayout and stackFromEnd on the LinearLayoutManager and maybe scroll to keep the same visual position. I will implement that.

For **local file** mode: currently reverseLayout=false, so adapter[0] at top = oldest (if file is oldest-first). So default: oldest at top. Reversed: we want newest at top. So we need reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true for file when reversed. And we don't need to change the list. So same thing: just flip reverseLayout and stackFromEnd.

So implementation:
1. Add a "sort order" icon button in design_logcat.xml (e.g. between export and close, or next to delete). Use an icon like "sort" or "swap vert".
2. In LogcatDesign: 
   - Add var reversedOrder: Boolean = false
   - In init, set click listener on the new button to send Request.ToggleSortOrder (or just toggle and apply)
   - Add Request.ToggleSortOrder
   - When ToggleSortOrder: reversedOrder = !reversedOrder; apply to layout manager (reverseLayout = if(streaming) !reversedOrder else reversedOrder; stackFromEnd = ...); and optionally scroll to keep sensible position
3. Apply layout: reverseLayout = (streaming && !reversedOrder) || (!streaming && reversedOrder); stackFromEnd = same. So: reversedOrder true → reverseLayout=true for file, reverseLayout=false for streaming. So reverseLayout = (streaming xor reversedOrder)? I will see: streaming, default (reversedOrder false): reverseLayout true. streaming, reversed: reverseLayout false. file, default: reverseLayout false. file, reversed: reverseLayout true. So reverseLayout = (streaming && !reversedOrder) || (!streaming && reversedOrder) = streaming xor reversedOrder? No: streaming true, reversed false → true. streaming true, reversed true → false. streaming false, reversed false → false. streaming false, reversed true → true. So reverseLayout = (streaming && !reversedOrder) || (!streaming && reversedOrder). And stackFromEnd we want true when reverseLayout is true (so that we start from end). So stackFromEnd = reverseLayout.
4. LogcatActivity: handle Request.ToggleSortOrder - but actually the Design can do it all: just toggle the variable and update the LayoutManager. So we don't need to pass to Activity. So Request.ToggleSortOrder is handled inside Design: we don't need to send to activity. So the button just toggles local state and updates the RecyclerView's layout manager.

I will re-read the layout code. We have:
binding.recyclerList.layoutManager = LinearLayoutManager(context).apply {
    if (streaming) {
        reverseLayout = true
        stackFromEnd = true
    }
}
So for streaming we set reverseLayout and stackFromEnd once in init. To make it toggleable we need to either replace the layout manager or update its reverseLayout/stackFromEnd. LinearLayoutManager has setReverseLayout(boolean) and setStackFromEnd(boolean) - I need to check. Actually in Android, LinearLayoutManager has reverseLayout and stackFromEnd properties. We can get the layout manager and set these. So in the click handler we get binding.recyclerList.layoutManager as LinearLayoutManager, then set reverseLayout and stackFromEnd, then maybe requestLayout or the RecyclerView will update. I will check - yes, we can do (binding.recyclerList.layoutManager as LinearLayoutManager).reverseLayout = newValue.

So no need for a Request to Activity - we can handle entirely in Design. So:
1. design_logcat.xml: add ImageView for sort order (icon: ic_baseline_swap_vert or similar)
2. LogcatDesign: 
   - var reversedOrder = false
   - In init, find the new view and setOnClickListener { toggleSortOrder() }
   - fun toggleSortOrder() { reversedOrder = !reversedOrder; applySortOrder() }
   - fun applySortOrder() { (binding.recyclerList.layoutManager as? LinearLayoutManager)?.apply { reverseLayout = (streaming != reversedOrder); stackFromEnd = reverseLayout } }  Wait, reverseLayout = (streaming != reversedOrder): streaming true, reversed false → true. streaming true, reversed true → false. Good. streaming false, reversed false → false. streaming false, reversed true → true. Good. So reverseLayout = (streaming != reversedOrder). And stackFromEnd = reverseLayout to keep the same behavior when we use reverse layout.
3. We need to scroll after toggling so the user doesn't get lost - e.g. scroll to position 0 or to the end. When we flip, scrolling to position 0 might show different content. I will just apply the layout and not scroll - user can scroll. Or scroll to top (position 0). So after apply, binding.recyclerList.scrollToPosition(0). That way they see the "top" of the new order.

- streaming=true, reversedOrder=true: reverseLayout=false, stackFromEnd=false → oldest at top. ✓
- streaming=false (file), reversedOrder=false: reverseLayout=false → oldest at top (index 0 = first in file). ✓

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I need to fix the init block: we're calling `applySortOrder(false)` on the LinearLayoutManager but that's an extension function that takes a Boolean. So we need to call it as `applySortOrder(false)` - but the extension is on LinearLayoutManager and we're inside `apply { }` so `this` is the LinearLayoutManager. So we need to call `applySortOrder(false)` - but applySortOrder is defined as `private fun LinearLayoutManager.applySortOrder(scrollToTop: Boolean)` and it uses `binding.recyclerList.scrollToPosition(0)`. So when we're in init, we're inside `LinearLayoutManager(context).apply { applySortOrder(false) }` - so `this` is the manager, and we call this.applySortOrder(false). Good. But wait - in applySortOrder we use `binding.recyclerList.scrollToPosition(0)` when scrollToTop is true. When scrollToTop is false we don't scroll. And we set reverseLayout and stackFromEnd. So the init is correct.

I will double-check: in init we have
binding.recyclerList.layoutManager = LinearLayoutManager(context).apply {
    applySortOrder(false)
}
So we're calling applySortOrder on the newly created LinearLayoutManager with scrollToTop=false. So reverseLayout = streaming != reversedOrder. Initially reversedOrder = false. So reverseLayout = streaming != false = streaming. So when streaming=true, reverseLayout=true, stackFromEnd=true. When streaming=false, reverseLayout=false, stackFromEnd=false. Good.

Read lints to make sure there are no errors.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

There's an issue: in `applySortOrder(true)` we're calling it without a receiver - we need to call it on the layout manager. So we should do:
binding.recyclerList.layoutManager?.let { (it as? LinearLayoutManager)?.applySortOrder(true) }
or we could change applySortOrder to not be an extension and instead take the manager and the flag. I will change it so that toggleSortOrder gets the layout manager and calls applySortOrder on it:

private fun toggleSortOrder() {
    reversedOrder = !reversedOrder
    (binding.recyclerList.layoutManager as? LinearLayoutManager)?.applySortOrder(true)
}

That way we're calling applySortOrder on the layout manager. Good.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

patchMessages has logic that scrolls when streaming and isTop: scrollToPosition(messages.size - 1). When we're in reversed order (oldest at top), we might want to scroll to 0 to show newest... Actually when reversedOrder is true, reverseLayout is false, so the list shows oldest at top (position 0) and newest at bottom. So when we append new messages, they go to the end (bottom). So "stick to bottom" in that case would be scrollToPosition(messages.size - 1). When reversedOrder is false (streaming default), we have reverseLayout=true, so newest is at top (position 0 in visual terms - actually position 0 is at bottom with reverseLayout). So when we append, we want to scroll to show the new message. The current code scrolls to messages.size - 1 when isTop. With reverseLayout=true, position (size-1) is at top (newest). So that's correct. When reversedOrder=true, reverseLayout=false, so position 0 is at top (oldest), position size-1 at bottom (newest). So when we append, we want to scroll to bottom to see newest, i.e. scrollToPosition(messages.size - 1). So the existing patchMessages scroll logic is still correct - we scroll to the last position to show newest. No change needed there.

I will add the string to other locale files if they have logcat-related strings so the app doesn't fall back to default. I will check values-zh-rTW, values-ja, etc.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


已在 Logcat 界面加上“倒序”功能，修改如下：

**1. 界面（`design_logcat.xml`）**  
- 在导出按钮和关闭按钮之间增加一个图标按钮（`sort_order_view`），使用 `ic_baseline_swap_vert`，`contentDescription` 为“倒序 / Reverse order”。

**2. 逻辑（`LogcatDesign.kt`）**  
- 增加状态：`reversedOrder`（默认 `false`）。  
- **流式（实时）Logcat**：  
  - 默认：`reverseLayout = true`、`stackFromEnd = true` → 最新在顶部。  
  - 点击后：`reverseLayout = false`、`stackFromEnd = false` → 最旧在顶部。  
- **本地文件**：  
  - 默认：正向布局 → 最旧在顶部。  
  - 点击后：反向布局 → 最新在顶部。  
- 点击按钮时：`reversedOrder = !reversedOrder`，更新 `LinearLayoutManager` 的 `reverseLayout` 和 `stackFromEnd`，并 `scrollToPosition(0)`，让用户看到当前顺序的“顶部”。

**3. 文案**  
- 在 `values/strings.xml` 中增加 `log_reverse_order`（"Reverse order"）。  
- 在 `values-zh`、`values-zh-rTW`、`values-zh-rHK` 中为“倒序”；`values-ja`“逆順”；`values-ko`“역순”；`values-ru`“Обратный порядок”；`values-vi`“Đảo thứ tự”。

效果：在 Logcat（流式或本地文件）界面点击该按钮，日志的时间顺序会在“最新在上”和“最旧在上”之间切换，每次切换后列表会滚到当前顺序的顶部。

---

_**User**_

每次结束日志的时候，会加入一些貌似无关的信息； 
例如： info dns:
info enable: true
info prefer-h3"true 等等

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is saying that when ending/finishing the log (probably when stopping the logcat or when the Clash service stops), some seemingly irrelevant information gets added to the log - for example:
- info dns:
- info enable: true
- info prefer-h3"true
etc.

This sounds like when the log stream ends or when switching profiles/stopping the service, the core or some component is writing configuration or state info as "log" messages, which then appear in the logcat view. The user wants to either:
1. Filter these out from the log display, or
2. Find where these messages are being emitted and stop them from being logged in this context

I will search for where such log messages might be coming from - "info dns", "info enable", "prefer-h3" etc. This could be in the core (Go), in the Android app's log writing, or in how logcat captures system logs.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


正在查找这些 “info dns”、“info enable”、“prefer-h3” 等信息的来源。

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I found the source. In `core/src/main/golang/native/config/load.go`:

1. `Load(path string)` is called when loading a profile (e.g. when starting or switching).
2. It calls `logDns(rawCfg)` which:
   - Marshals the DNS config to YAML
   - Logs `log.Infoln("dns:")` 
   - Then for each line of the YAML, logs `log.Infoln("  %s", line)` - so we get lines like "  enable: true", "  prefer-h3: true", etc.

So when the user "ends" the log (stops the service or switches profile), the core might be reloading or the Load is triggered, and this dumps the entire DNS config as info-level log lines into the logcat. The user finds this irrelevant noise.

**Fix:** Remove or downgrade this logging. Options:
1. Remove the `logDns` call entirely from `Load()` - then we never dump DNS config to the log.
2. Change `logDns` to use `log.Debugln` instead of `log.Infoln` - so it only appears when log level is debug.
3. Remove just the `logDns(rawCfg)` call from `Load()` so we don't dump DNS on every load.

The user said "每次结束日志的时候" - every time when ending the log. So it could be:
- When stopping the Clash service (which might trigger some cleanup/reload that calls Load or similar)
- Or when closing the logcat UI after viewing a log file

Actually re-reading again: "每次结束日志的时候，会加入一些貌似无关的信息" - when (we) end the log each time, some seemingly irrelevant information gets added. So the sequence is: user is viewing the log (streaming or file), then when they "end" (close/stop) the log, extra lines like "info dns:", "info enable: true", "info prefer-h3"true" appear. That strongly suggests that when the user taps "Close" on the logcat screen, the app stops the LogcatService and perhaps the Clash service or profile gets reloaded/restarted, and that triggers `Load()` in the core, which calls `logDns()` and dumps DNS config as Infoln. Those lines then get captured by the logcat or written to the same log stream.

So the fix is to stop dumping the DNS config to the log. The cleanest approach is to remove the `logDns` call from `Load()`, or change `logDns` to use Debug level so it doesn't clutter the normal log. I'll remove the call to `logDns(rawCfg)` from `Load()` so that loading a config no longer dumps the entire DNS section to the log. If we want to keep it for debugging, we could use `log.Debugln` inside `logDns` instead; I'll go with removing the call so the log stays clean.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


已经改好了。

**原因：**  
在 `core/src/main/golang/native/config/load.go` 里，每次加载配置时都会调用 `logDns(rawCfg)`，把整段 DNS 配置用 `log.Infoln` 打出来，例如：
- `info dns:`
- `info   enable: true`
- `info   prefer-h3: true`
- 以及其它 DNS 相关行

结束日志或重启/切换配置时会触发 `Load()`，这些行就会混进日志里。

**修改：**  
1. 删除了 `logDns` 函数（不再把 DNS 配置当 info 日志输出）。  
2. 在 `Load()` 里去掉了对 `logDns(rawCfg)` 的调用。  
3. 移除了因此不再使用的 import：`"strings"` 和 `"github.com/metacubex/mihomo/common/yaml"`。

之后结束日志或重载配置时，不会再出现这些 “info dns:”、“info enable: true”、“info prefer-h3”true” 等无关信息。

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I found the fallback group logic. I will read the fallback.go file in full to understand how it selects nodes and when it uses timeout. The user says timeout is 250, failure-reset-interval is 3000, and they haven't manually selected any node - so theoretically when a node exceeds 250ms it should trigger fallback, but it doesn't (nodes at 600ms don't trigger).

From the grep:
- fallback.go line 151-158: timeoutMs := f.selectedTimeout; if timeoutMs <= 0 { timeoutMs = f.TestTimeout }; if timeoutMs <= 0 { timeoutMs = 5000 }; ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(...)

So it uses selectedTimeout first, then TestTimeout, then default 5000. The config has "timeout" (TestTimeout) and "selected-timeout" (SelectedTimeout). The user said timeout is 250 - that would be the group's "timeout" which maps to TestTimeout. And they didn't manually select - so selectedTimeout might be 0 or not set. So timeoutMs should become f.TestTimeout = 250. So the context timeout should be 250ms. But the user says it's not triggering fallback even at 600ms.

I need to read the full fallback.go to see:
1. How the proxy is chosen (first available? does it check delay before using?)
2. When does it actually use the timeout - is it only when dialing, or is there a delay check?
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I see the issue now. In `findAliveProxy`, when `len(f.selected) == 0` (no manual selection), it iterates over proxies and returns the first one where `proxy.AliveForTestUrl(f.testUrl)` is true. So it's using **AliveForTestUrl** - not checking the **delay** against the timeout. So even if a node has 600ms delay, if it's "alive" (test succeeded at some point), it will be used. The **timeout** (250ms) is only used when:
1. In Set() - when manually selecting a node, for the URLTest
2. In findAliveProxy when f.selected != "" - for checking if the selected node is still alive with selectedTimeout

So the bug is: when there's **no manual selection**, fallback uses `AliveForTestUrl(f.testUrl)` which doesn't take the timeout into account - it just checks if the proxy was ever marked alive (or recently passed a test). The group's **timeout** (TestTimeout=250) is never used when auto-picking! We need to find what AliveForTestUrl does and whether we need to add a delay check.

I will search for AliveForTestUrl and how delay is stored/checked.
</details></think>

I found it. In fallback.go `findAliveProxy`, when there's no manual selection (`len(f.selected) == 0`), it only checks `proxy.AliveForTestUrl(f.testUrl)` - it does NOT check whether the proxy's delay is within the timeout (250ms). And we need to add a check: when no manual selection, only consider a proxy "alive" for fallback if it's alive AND (optionally) its delay is within TestTimeout. I will check constant/adapters.go for the Proxy interface and adapter/adapter.go for AliveForTestUrl.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I understand the full picture:

1. **Fallback findAliveProxy** (no manual selection): It iterates proxies and returns the **first** one where `proxy.AliveForTestUrl(f.testUrl)` is true. It does **NOT** check whether the proxy's delay is within the group's `TestTimeout` (250ms).

2. So a node with 600ms delay is still "alive" (the URL test succeeded at some point), and it gets returned as the first alive proxy. The **timeout** (250ms) is only used in fallback when:
   - Manually selecting a node (Set) - for the URLTest
   - When checking if the manually selected node is still alive - with selectedTimeout

3. **Fix**: When there's no manual selection (`len(f.selected) == 0`), we should only consider a proxy "alive" for fallback if:
   - `proxy.AliveForTestUrl(f.testUrl)` is true, AND
   - `proxy.LastDelayForTestUrl(f.testUrl) <= timeout` (where timeout is f.TestTimeout, or default 5000)

So we need to add a delay check in findAliveProxy for the no-selection case: use TestTimeout as the max acceptable delay. If LastDelayForTestUrl > TestTimeout, treat the proxy as not alive for this purpose and try the next one.

I will implement this in fallback.go.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


发现问题：在无手动选择时，fallback 仅用 `AliveForTestUrl` 判断，未检查延迟是否在 `timeout`（250ms）内，导致 600ms 的节点仍被当作可用。正在修改 fallback：在无选择时仅当 `AliveForTestUrl` 为真且 `LastDelayForTestUrl <= TestTimeout` 时才视为可用。

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I need to handle the case where all proxies exceed the timeout - we should still return the first proxy (or the first alive one?) so that traffic doesn't fail. Looking at the original code: if no proxy passes the check, it falls through and returns `proxies[0]` at the end. So with my change, if all proxies have delay > 250ms, we'll iterate all, none will match, and we return proxies[0]. That might be a node with 600ms - which is correct fallback behavior: if no node is within timeout, use the first one anyway (or we could return the first "alive" one regardless of delay). The user said "节点超过 250 就会触发 fallback" - so they want to fall through to the next node when current exceeds 250. So we're good: we skip nodes with delay > 250 and use the next one; if all exceed 250 we still return proxies[0] at the end.

Actually re-reading again: "理论上应该节点超过 250 就会触发 fallback" - when a node exceeds 250ms, fallback should be triggered (i.e. try the next node). So we skip nodes that are alive but have delay > 250, and pick the first one that is alive AND delay <= 250. If none, we return proxies[0]. Good.

I will double-check: LastDelayForTestUrl returns uint16. If the delay is 600ms, that's 600. uint16 can hold it. And we compare with timeoutMs 250. So 600 <= 250 is false, we skip. Good.
</details></think>

- We only return a proxy if it's alive AND LastDelayForTestUrl <= 250.
- So a node with 600ms will be skipped and we try the next one.

_**Agent (default)**_


修改已经按预期生效，总结如下：

**原因：**  
在 `fallback.go` 的 `findAliveProxy` 里，当**没有手动选择**（`len(f.selected) == 0`）时，只判断了 `proxy.AliveForTestUrl(f.testUrl)`。只要某次测速成功，节点就会被当成“存活”，**没有**再根据 `timeout`（250ms）过滤延迟。所以即使节点已经 600ms，只要曾经测通过，仍会被当作第一个可用节点使用，不会往下 fallback。

**修改：**  
在无手动选择的分支里，除了“存活”外，再按组里的 `timeout` 做延迟过滤：

1. 使用 `f.TestTimeout` 作为最大允许延迟（≤0 时用 5000ms）。
2. 仅当 `proxy.AliveForTestUrl(f.testUrl)` 且 `proxy.LastDelayForTestUrl(f.testUrl) <= timeoutMs` 时才返回该节点。
3. 若当前节点延迟 > 250ms，则跳过并继续下一个；若所有节点都超过 250ms，仍会返回 `proxies[0]`，保持“总得选一个”的 fallback 行为。

这样在 timeout=250、failure-reset-interval=3000 且未手动选节点时，延迟超过 250ms 的节点会被跳过，fallback 会按顺序尝试下一个节点。

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is saying that every time they reopen the proxy (screen?), a fixed node is always selected - they think this might be a bug. So the expected behavior might be: when opening the proxy screen, it should show the current selected node (or auto-selected node) from the core, not always the same fixed one.

I need to understand:
1. How does the proxy screen get the "current" selection when it opens?
2. Is there some state (e.g. uiStore.proxyLastGroup or a saved "selected" node) that always restores to a fixed node instead of reflecting what the core actually has?

I will search for how proxy selection is loaded/restored when opening the proxy screen - ProxyActivity, ProxyDesign, and any store that saves "last group" or selected node.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I see. The UI has:
1. `proxyLastGroup` - saved in UiStore, used to remember which **tab** (proxy group) was last viewed. When opening proxy screen, it uses `groupNames.indexOf(uiStore.proxyLastGroup)` to set the initial ViewPager position.
2. When Reload is handled, the **selected node** for each group comes from `queryProxyGroup` - the core returns `group.now` (current selection). So `state.now = group.now` - the UI gets the current selection from the core.

So the "fixed node" issue might not be about which **tab** is shown, but about which **node** is selected within each group. If every time they reopen the proxy, the core always returns the same "now" for selector/fallback/url-test groups - that could be:
1. Core side: selector/fallback is persisting or defaulting to a fixed node (e.g. first in list) when the app restarts or profile reloads.
2. Or the UI is not showing what the core returns - e.g. some stale state.

I will check: when does the core's selector/fallback "selection" get reset? Maybe when the app process is killed and restarted, or when the Clash service restarts, the core re-initializes and selector groups default to the first node? And perhaps there's persist/session override that should restore selection but doesn't?

I will search for where selector/fallback "now" or selection is persisted or initialized in the core.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the "now" comes from `g.Now()` - the ProxyGroup's current selection. When the app is closed and reopened, or when the Clash service restarts (e.g. profile reload), the core re-creates the proxy groups from config. The selector/fallback/urltest groups have an in-memory `selected` field that starts as empty (or first proxy for selector - I saw in selector.go `selected: "COMPATIBLE"` in MarshalJSON default). So when the core **restarts** (new process, new config load), all groups get fresh state: selector might default to first proxy, fallback to empty (auto), url-test to empty. So "every time reopen proxy" could mean:
1. User closes the proxy **screen** (activity) and reopens it - the core is still running, so g.Now() should still be what the user selected. Unless...
2. User said "每次重新打开代理" - could mean "every time I reopen the proxy (app/screen)" or "every time I restart the proxy (Clash service)". If it's (1), the state is in the core; if the core is the same process, selection should persist. If it's (2), when Clash restarts, the core doesn't persist selector/fallback selection - so it would reset to default (first node or auto). So the fix might be: **persist** the proxy group selections (selector/fallback/urltest "now") and **apply** them when the core loads config. I will check if there's already a persist override that includes proxy selection. I saw config/process.go "Apply persist override" and "Apply session override" - I will read that.
</details></think>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So in the foss executor:
1. `updateProfile(cfg)` is called when config is updated.
2. If `profileCfg.StoreSelected` is true, it calls `patchSelectGroup(cfg.Proxies)`.
3. `patchSelectGroup` reads `cachefile.Cache().SelectedMap()` - a mapping of group name -> selected proxy name - and for each selector/fallback/urltest, calls `selector.ForceSet(selected)`.

But in the CFA (Clash for Android) native config process.go we have:
```go
func patchProfile(cfg *config.RawConfig, _ string) error {
	cfg.Profile.StoreSelected = false  // <-- CFA forces this to false!
	cfg.Profile.StoreFakeIP = true
	return nil
}
```
So CFA sets **StoreSelected = false**, which means when the config is applied, `updateProfile` will NOT call `patchSelectGroup`, so the core never restores the selected proxy from any cache! And the cache might be written by the desktop version (cachefile) but on Android there might be no cachefile or it's not used.

So the bug could be:
1. CFA sets StoreSelected = false, so selections are never restored from cache.
2. When the user "reopens proxy" - if they mean reopening the app or the Clash service restarts - the core loads config, creates fresh proxy groups (selector defaults to first node, fallback to auto), and never applies any saved selection. So they always see the "fixed" (default) node.

The fix could be:
1. Use persist override to store proxy group selections (e.g. {"proxy-groups":{"GROUP_NAME":"selected_node"}}) and apply them when loading config. But the RawConfig merge might not work that way - we need to check how override is merged.
2. Or: after the core loads config (ApplyConfig), the Android app could read the persist override for proxy selections and call PatchSelector for each group. So we need to:
   - When user selects a node (PatchSelector), also write the selection to persist override (e.g. a "proxy-selections" or merge into existing override).
   - When config is loaded (after Load()), read the persist override and call PatchSelector for each saved group->node.

I will check the override structure - what does the app currently put in persist override? And how does the config merge work?
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

ConfigurationOverride doesn't have a "proxy-groups" or "selected" map for storing proxy group selections. So when the user selects a node, the app only calls patchSelector (which updates the in-memory state in the core) but doesn't persist it anywhere. When the user "reopens proxy" - if they mean:
1. Just close and reopen the ProxyActivity - the core is still running, so the selection should still be there. Unless the core is being recreated (e.g. service killed and restarted)?
2. Or "reopen proxy" = restart Clash (stop and start) - then the core process restarts, config is reloaded, and proxy groups get fresh state (selector defaults to first node). So the selection is lost.

So the fix should be: **Persist proxy group selections** and **restore them when config is loaded**.

Options:
A) Store selections in persist override. The RawConfig in mihomo uses `json` tags and the override is merged via `json.NewDecoder(...).Decode(cfg)` - so we'd need to add a field that the config parser understands. Looking at executor.go, it uses `cachefile.Cache().SelectedMap()` - that's a separate cache file, not the override. So the config's proxy-groups might support a "selected" in each group when parsing. I will check how proxy-groups are parsed and if they have a "selected" field.

B) After config is loaded in native (Load(path)), we could call a new native function that applies saved proxy selections. So we need: 1) A place to store selections (persist override could have a new key "proxy-selections": {"GroupName": "node1"}), 2) When Load() finishes, read that and call PatchSelector for each. But the override is applied *before* parsing (patchOverride runs in process()), so the RawConfig is merged with override. So if we put proxy-groups in the override with selected names, we need the RawConfig structure to have proxy-groups and the parser to set the selected. I will check the foss config RawConfig for proxy-groups structure.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I see - the foss executor uses `profile.Cache().SelectedMap()` from cachefile. So on desktop, the cache file stores the selected map. CFA sets StoreSelected = false in process.go, so updateProfile never calls patchSelectGroup. So when config is applied, no selection is restored.

The fix: we need to either:
1. Enable StoreSelected and make the cachefile work on Android (complex - cachefile might write to a path that's not available on Android).
2. Or: store proxy selections in persist override (e.g. a new key in the override JSON that we merge into RawConfig before parsing). The config's ProxyGroup is []map[string]any - so when we merge override, we could have "proxy-groups" in override that patches the proxy-groups array. But that's complex because we'd need to match by group name and set "selected" in each group map.
3. Or: After config is loaded (in native Load()), after hub.ApplyConfig(cfg), we read the persist override for a "proxy-selections" or similar, and call PatchSelector for each. So we need: (a) When user selects a node (in app, when PatchSelector is called), also update persist override with the new selection. (b) The persist override format - we could add a new key "proxy-selections": {"GROUP_A": "node1", "GROUP_B": "node2"}. (c) In native, when Load() is called, after ApplyConfig, read override, parse proxy-selections, and call PatchSelector for each. But wait - the override is applied in process() which runs during UnmarshalAndPatch, before Parse(). So the override is merged into RawConfig. So if we add "proxy-groups" to the override with each group having "selected": "node_name", we need to check if the config parser uses that. I will check outboundgroup parser for "selected" in the group option.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The GroupCommonOption doesn't have a "selected" field - so the config file doesn't pass initial selection when parsing. The selection is only applied later by patchSelectGroup in executor when StoreSelected is true, which reads from cachefile.

So the cleanest fix for CFA is:
1. **Store proxy selections** when user selects a node - we need to persist them. We can add to persist override a new key like "proxy-selections": {"GroupName": "node1"}. But ConfigurationOverride (Kotlin) doesn't have proxy-selections - and the override is applied in native by json.Decode into RawConfig. So we need to add support in native: when we ReadOverride, we get a JSON object. We could add a separate file or a key in the same override. Actually looking at patchOverride - it does `json.NewDecoder(strings.NewReader(ReadOverride(...))).Decode(cfg)` - so it decodes the override JSON **into the RawConfig** (cfg). So the override can contain any RawConfig fields. RawConfig has ProxyGroup []map[string]any. So if we merge override that has "proxy-groups": [{"name": "GLOBAL", "selected": "Node1"}, ...], the merge might replace or merge. Actually json.Decode into a struct - it would overwrite the field. So if override has "proxy-groups": [...], it would replace the entire proxy-groups from the config. That's wrong - we only want to patch the "selected" in each group.

So we need a different approach:
2. **Store selections in a separate place** (e.g. a key in override that we don't merge into RawConfig). When native Load() finishes (after ApplyConfig), we read the persist override, extract "proxy-selections" if any, and for each group call PatchSelector. And when the app calls patchSelector (Bridge), we also write the new selection to persist override. So we need: (a) Persist override format to include "proxy-selections": {"GROUP_A": "node1"}. (b) When writing override (from app), the app currently only writes ConfigurationOverride - which is a Kotlin model. So when would we write proxy-selections? When user selects a node in ProxyActivity - we call patchSelector but we don't have a path to update persist override with the selection. So we need the **core/native** to do it: when PatchSelector is called, native could also update the persist override to add this selection. So in native tunnel proxies.go PatchSelector, after s.Set(name), we could read the current persist override, merge in the new selection, and write it back. (c) When Load() is called in native config/load.go, after hub.ApplyConfig(cfg), we need to read persist override, get proxy-selections, and call PatchSelector for each. But Load() is in config package and PatchSelector is in tunnel package - we need to avoid circular import. So we could do the "apply proxy selections after load" in the same place that calls Load() - that's probably in the service or in native main. I will check where Load() is called.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So Load() is called from config.go (the JNI bridge). After hub.ApplyConfig(cfg), we could apply proxy selections. We need:
1. A way to store proxy selections - in the persist override JSON. The persist override is read in process() and merged into RawConfig. So the override is a JSON that gets decoded into RawConfig. If we add a key "proxy-selections" to the override, when we Decode into RawConfig, it would try to set RawConfig.ProxySelections - but RawConfig doesn't have that field, so it would be ignored. So we need to either: (a) Not merge proxy-selections into RawConfig, but read it separately. So we'd read the override file/content twice - once in process() for normal override (without proxy-selections?), and once after Load() to get proxy-selections and apply. Actually the override is ReadOverride(OverrideSlotPersist) - it returns a string (JSON). So we could parse it in Load() after ApplyConfig, extract "proxy-selections" (if we add it when writing), and call PatchSelector for each. So we need to **write** proxy-selections when the user selects a node. Where does the app write persist override? Only when the user changes override settings (OverrideSettingsActivity, MetaFeatureSettingsActivity). So when user selects a node in ProxyActivity, we call patchSelector (native) but we don't update the persist override. So the native side should: when PatchSelector is called, read the current persist override (it's a string), parse it as a generic map, add or update "proxy-selections" -> group -> name, write it back. And when Load() is called, after ApplyConfig, read persist override, parse, get "proxy-selections", and for each group call tunnel.PatchSelector. But wait - the persist override is written by the app (Kotlin) with ConfigurationOverride - which doesn't have proxy-selections. So when the app writes the override, it uses ConfigurationOverride which serializes to JSON. So the override file only contains the fields in ConfigurationOverride. We can't add proxy-selections from native without the app knowing. So we have two options: A) Add proxy-selections to ConfigurationOverride (Kotlin model) and have the app update it when user selects a node (read override, update proxySelections, write override). Then in native process() or Load(), we need to apply proxy-selections. But process() decodes override into RawConfig - RawConfig doesn't have ProxySelections. So we'd need to decode the override in Load() after ApplyConfig, but the override is the same JSON - we need a struct that has both the override fields and proxy-selections. So in native we could have a struct for the full override that includes ProxySelections map[string]string, and when we ReadOverride we parse into that, apply the rest to RawConfig in process(), and after Load() we apply ProxySelections by calling PatchSelector. So process() would need to use a struct that has all override fields that get merged into RawConfig, plus we'd store proxy-selections. When writing override from app, the app would need to include proxy-selections in the JSON. So we need: 1. ConfigurationOverride (Kotlin) to add proxySelections: Map<String, String>? = null 2. When user selects a node in ProxyActivity, after patchSelector, also queryOverride(Persist), update proxySelections (merge in the new selection for that group), patchOverride(Persist, updated) 3. In native process(), when we decode the override, we need to not lose proxy-selections. Currently we do json.NewDecoder(ReadOverride(Persist)).Decode(cfg) - we decode into RawConfig (cfg). RawConfig doesn't have proxy-selections, so json would ignore it. So when we later read the override in Load() to get proxy-selections, we'd get the full JSON including proxy-selections. So we need in Load() after ApplyConfig: read override string, parse as map[string]any or a struct that has ProxySelections map[string]string, then for each k,v call tunnel.PatchSelector(k, v). And when does the app write the override with proxy-selections? When the user selects a node - we need to read override, add the selection, write back. So the app side: in ProxyActivity when handling Request.Select, after withClash { patchSelector(...) }, we need to also update persist override: queryOverride(Persist), set proxySelections[groupName] = nodeName (we need to get the group name - it's names[it.index]), patchOverride(Persist, updated). So we need ConfigurationOverride to have proxySelections. I will check the Kotlin ConfigurationOverride again - it uses @SerialName. So we add @SerialName("proxy-selections") var proxySelections: Map<String, String>? = null. Then in ProxyActivity after patchSelector, we update the override. And in native Load(), after hub.ApplyConfig(cfg), we read the override, parse it - but we're in Go. We need to get the proxy-selections from the override. The override is applied in process() by decoding into RawConfig. So the override JSON might have "proxy-selections": {"GLOBAL": "Node1"}. When we Decode(override, &rawConfig), the rawConfig doesn't have ProxySelections field so it's ignored. So we need in Load() to read the override again and parse just the proxy-selections. So in native config/load.go after ApplyConfig, we could call a new function ApplyProxySelections() which reads ReadOverride(OverrideSlotPersist), parses as json into a struct that has ProxySelections map[string]string, and for each group, name in ProxySelections calls tunnel.PatchSelector(group, name). But tunnel is in a different package - tunnel.PatchSelector. So we need to import "cfa/native/tunnel" in config package. I will check - config is in cfa/native/config, tunnel is in cfa/native/tunnel. So we can import "cfa/native/tunnel" and call tunnel.PatchSelector. But wait - that might create a circular dependency if tunnel imports config. I will check - tunnel.go probably doesn't import config. So we're good. So the plan: 1. Add to ConfigurationOverride (Kotlin): proxySelections: Map<String, String>? = null with @SerialName("proxy-selections") 2. In ProxyActivity, when handling Request.Select: after withClash { patchSelector(names[it.index], it.name) }, also: val override = withClash { queryOverride(Clash.OverrideSlot.Persist) }; override.proxySelections = (override.proxySelections ?: emptyMap()) + (names[it.index] to it.name); withClash { patchOverride(Clash.OverrideSlot.Persist, override) }. 3. In native config/load.go, after hub.ApplyConfig(cfg), call a function that reads persist override, parses proxy-selections, and calls tunnel.PatchSelector for each. 4. We need a Go struct for the override that includes ProxySelections. We can define a simple struct in load.go or override.go: type persistOverride struct { ProxySelections map[string]string `json:"proxy-selections"` } and the rest of the override is merged into RawConfig in process(). So when we read in Load(), we only need to parse proxy-selections. So we read the override string, decode into struct { ProxySelections map[string]string `json:"proxy-selections"` }, and if ProxySelections != nil, for group, name range ProxySelections { tunnel.PatchSelector(group, name) }. I will implement this.
</details></think>
